text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Before we get going here, I wanted to share with you something that you might find very useful and helpful.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Very recently, I wrote an ebook on how to make sure that you're billing at a high enough rate if you're a freelancer or consultant.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So if you do any type of freelancing or consulting, then please pay attention.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Very often when folks start consulting or freelancing, they set their rates too low, mainly because they don't know what the rate should be or they just feel like that's the best way to get clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And in fact, you will get clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: However, unfortunately, over a period of time, you'll find out that you just cannot sustain at that low consulting or freelancing rate.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So I've written a book, small ebook that you can download for free.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: It will show you the very bare minimum amount that you need to be charging as a freelancer or consultant so that you can make the right amount of money.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So if you decide to go full-time in consulting or full-time in freelancing, or just continue to do it when you have time, this will ensure that you're charging the right amount.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And you can get that ebook at donfelker.com slash ebook.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Just plug in your email.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: I'll send you the PDF right to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: It's free of charge.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So nothing on my end.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Just plug in your email.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And again, that's at donfelker.com slash ebook.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Everybody, welcome back to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Really glad to have you listening here to the podcast again.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Unfortunately, Kaushik's not with us again this week, but I do have a special guest that I'm really excited to talk to regarding a topic that I've never discussed before and never really dove into.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So hopefully you learn as much as I do here on today's podcast.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: On today's podcast, we have a special guest.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: His name is Glenn Liefite, and Glenn works at Microsoft.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Glenn, welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Thanks for having me, Don.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's great to be here.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, definitely.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So we've known each other for a number of years, all the way back to when I lived in the Twin Cities up in Minneapolis, and you were in the area too, when we spoke at code camps together and all kinds of stuff like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And we've stayed in contact, and you're over at Microsoft now.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And again, we've continued to communicate over the years.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: We recently started talking about some of the things that you do over at Microsoft, and then you mentioned something that was really interesting to me.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: But before we hop into that, can you give folks who are not familiar with you or anything around what you do, kind of background information about yourself and maybe software and kind of how you got into what you're doing and what you're doing now?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Yeah, sounds good.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, again, my name is Glenn Lifheit.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I've now been at Microsoft nine years.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But as I roll back the history a little bit, I actually don't have a technology degree.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm completely self-taught.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I kind of fell into it all when my best friend called me on the phone and said, yeah, so what are you doing today?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I said, I'm kind of bored at work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I was buying cars for an automotive fleet leasing company.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And he was starting up an internet service provider himself.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: This is in the mid-90s.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And he said, be careful what you ask for.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And by the next day, I had a job offer as he kind of figured it out.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And about a week later, I moved about 45 miles west to the town that I grew up in and helped set up an internet service provider for the first time.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it was a fascinating experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I was the first hired on employee.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it was quite the adventure.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I did everything from the role of CFO to, you know, all the technical work that we had to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But yeah, so I kind of got into technology from there, then kind of rolled through some technical support roles, moved into more of an operational person.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, you know, Windows, Unix, you know, kind of more OS based or web server based solutions.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Further and further, I kind of went in, developed for a little bit, really enjoyed that, then moved into more of an architectural type role.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And when I was at FICO, the credit score company in the Twin Cities, that's where I had the biggest set of pivots, where I was an architect, and then I became the security architect, and then I moved on to Microsoft.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: doing a myriad of different types of work, all in security and secure development for the most part.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The biggest things I helped roll out static code analysis to all of IT.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And to give you an idea, when I arrived at Microsoft, I moved from a seven-person or maybe nine-person security team to a security team of 790.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Whoa.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The scale is just 100-fold.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, as someone coming in, I'm like, holy cow.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It was such a different thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Everybody was so specialized, and you had to have such process and things like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I've learned a lot about navigating process, obviously, doing it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then the IT department at the last company was 40.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There were 25,000 developers in IT when I joined.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And in Microsoft, I had to rule, I rolled out static, you know, to a small company.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm like, yeah, I rolled out static code analysis to the IT group.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I rolled it out to 3,500 different teams as we rolled through it, you know, pushed beyond all the scaling limits, all sorts of interesting things as part of it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And did that for a while and then
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Also did a kind of a mini CISO role.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What is a CISO?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What does that mean?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I was assigned to one of the businesses.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So when you look at a business in Microsoft, they're large.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, they've got each one's roughly the size.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I usually say a very small Fortune 500 company.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They've got the budget.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They've got that many people.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They've got all that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so what they needed was people to help their leadership understand security.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so we would play that role of enabling their teams to make smart decisions from a security perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Some of the fun parts of that was I actually sat in the...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: in the monthly meetings that they had for all the approvals and everything else.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I sat with executive leadership as they went through those things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then as there were development teams that needed help from that perspective, like, oh, we're not going to approve that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We need to...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: To do that, I would dive deeper in with the development teams as well and do some training, help them understand where the issues were and how they could do better security design.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is it CSO?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is that what you said CSO?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: CSO is it Chief Security Officer?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Yeah, kind of Chief Information Security Officer is that most of the time in a company like our size, we're not...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We call it a mini role because we're not the real thing by any stretch.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We don't have the executive power by any means.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But what we do have is we kind of lead them from a security process across all of those things and all the other needs that they may have as a business.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Every business is very different, obviously.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so they have different risks that we take on.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Then I moved over to my current team, which is customer security and trust over at Microsoft.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We do a lot of fascinating work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We actually exist in the legal organization at Microsoft, which is an interesting place for an engineering team.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But it's actually a great place because we're centralized and we don't have any of the push and shove when it comes to timelines for a product.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we can concentrate on security and security only.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: looking at what needs to be done.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: How do we make it for the next generation?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then we work with each of the product groups to make it available for them.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because the thought process behind like Xbox is going to be different than the thought process behind Azure or different than the educational team.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, for example, we've got, you know, we've got an educational team and they do, we do, you know, software that is used by kindergartners.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You start thinking about passwords and, you know, the thought process of passwords for an adult and process of passwords for a kindergartner are a very different thought from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And identity is a very different thought process, you know, in the education market than in other markets as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I think it's, it's one of those things that, you know,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's a fun little thing that we get to do is be across Microsoft so we get to see all the different types of things, take care of security behind early stage acquisition work, do some work with internal audits.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it's just fun being able to work with all the different types of product groups that we have.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And essentially, if we see risk, we just dive in and help them resolve whatever that risk might be.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now, when we first started talking about what you had done back and forth over the last many months over at Microsoft, one of the things that popped up is you said that you talked something about secure development lifecycle.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And that's why I wanted to bring you on today because that sounded very interesting.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: What is secure development lifecycle?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Well, it's morphed over the years.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now you'll oftentimes hear SecDevOps are some of those types of terms.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's kind of where it has come from.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The SDL, a lot of the SDL came actually out of Microsoft back in the earlier days when Bill Gates actually shut down all the development and said, you're only working on security issues.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and only those.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then we actually at Microsoft spun up a team, which is kind of an ancestor team to the team I'm currently on, that really helped build what became kind of what more of an industry standard secure development lifecycle.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the idea basically is for a secure development lifecycle is really just enabling security steps within the development process to enable secure code.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: In the grand scheme of things,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One of the issues from a security slash development relationship perspective always has been, you know, you've got the business context and all the business requirements that, you know, there's never a shortage of, to say the least.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then, you know, there's also, and those users care about quality.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They care about speed.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So all of those types of things tend to get baked in into requirements.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But security hasn't always been in that forefront.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so that's kind of where the role that we played is helping enable them.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And the other part is a lot of the security things are kind of hidden in terms of what's there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you need to make sure that you're doing the right thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it's not really a direct business person's role to be able to say, hey, I know you need A, B, C, and D, because they don't understand the technical risks that are underpinning.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They're an expert on the business.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So they'll help you with business logic flaws, but they won't necessarily help you with security flaws.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And that's essentially what this is, is to help enable putting things in place to catch things that you're going to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Sometimes, you know, for great examples are...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: that we often still run into these days is just people doing patterns that don't have, for example, input validation in place and things like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's still pretty prevalent across the industry.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's better and better because frameworks now have it built in more now than in previous years and continues to get better, but still doing it in a strict enough way because you still get significant numbers of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One of the things we're dealing with is the fact that the same vulnerabilities that we saw 10 years ago really are still, they're slightly different, but they're not really, the top 10 are really pretty much the same.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's a couple of new added ones because of mobile and frameworks that have gone out and the lack of Shockwave and some of those other frameworks that have disappeared.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's other things that have come up, but the vast majority of them are simple.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But we really start back with the SDL.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The idea is to start with secure design and then flow you all the way through a secure launch.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So what does a secure design look like from that perspective?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Let's say I have a development team and we're going to be building some products or we get some new features that we need to build from the business.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: What does that look like from the design perspective in a security sense?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Yeah, so there's two things to start with.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The first thing I'll caveat is there's a lot of things you can do, but you have to select the right things for you as an organization to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because if you're a team of two, you can do so much.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you're a team of 20,000, you can add more resources and do different things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: From that perspective, I tend to speak more right now from the volume perspective of quantity of employees and bandwidth, but we still have the same push and shove of...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Business features are there and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We probably have more security knowledge in-house, but from a bandwidth perspective, it's harder.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: As I look at what does secure design look like, really...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It starts with essentially the way you guys do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: As a development team, you might do data flow and work through that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Really what we do is what's called a threat model.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's really based off of your data flow and your applications.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's more in-depth necessarily than that, but you have to start somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so where I usually recommend Teams is baby steps approach because if I tell you, hey, by the way, let's go ahead and...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: model your entire application and it's been around for five years well that's a there's some laundry there to to get through to be able to to get that done so really what we do is we'll start let's start with fresh with with something clean you know so you start with basics okay what
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: what information does this application have in it?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What type of data do we have?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Who would be interested in that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Help us understand what that footprint looks like.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The other piece would be that, so really the first threat models I see actually start in Excel and it's just lists of information, which is where to start with.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because then what you need to be able to do is start understanding where the risk is.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then when we look broader at what a real threat model looks like or a larger scale threat model, it's really a data flow diagram with special coding on it that'll tell me
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: OK, I'm using this.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: This is, you know, this line is encrypted coming from here to here.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so we're using HTTPS.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And this is how we're authenticating.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We know that we have authentication in this place and it's done with certificates or it's done with whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then we can identify what that is.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it really is kind of, you know, managing of that application flow and the data to ensure that you understand what's trusted in your application, what's not trusted.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I think that's one of the key interesting points that we always run into is that as we look at things that are the information that you want to look at, it's what do you trust?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The question is, you know, because there's many pieces to that puzzle.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, do you trust that open source software?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, good question.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Should you trust that open source software?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And that's a whole hour discussion all in itself, probably, as we look at that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But then there's, do you trust the team down the hall that's doing the... Are they following the secure development lifecycle as much as you are?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Where do you draw that line as an organization to do that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you're a small company, it's a lot easier to draw that trust diagram because you know more about what's in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: When you're a good identification of something at Microsoft, we may have core components that are built that are...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: completely two or three organizations away, you don't even know the people involved in it necessarily.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You just know you consume the service of it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you have to make this, yeah, it's a black box to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you really have to make those assumptions.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And a lot of companies are that way.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you really have to understand, can you make assumptions?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Do I trust what they're doing is secure so that I can carry it forward?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: In the grand plan, in an ideal world, you'd be all having those security discussions already between the teams, and then that's much less of an issue.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But if you're just getting started, then you have to start with what you trust, and then you can kind of move out from that thing as you do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Let's say that you were coming into, let's say I had a company, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And I hired you.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And we are building the next generation, best note-taking app that ever happened in the world.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And it's got a mobile app and it's got an API.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: talking to me or my team what would you kind of talk to us about to make sure that we had our bases covered from the initial outset of before we kind of started running before we started writing code or getting too deep down the feature set right right i think that you know as we look at i think there's a set of expectations that you need to set for yourself as an organization
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: especially if you're developing something new.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: First is like, okay, how secure are we making this?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, are we designing this to the DOD can be a customer or are we designing this so the traditional end user is using it?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because there's vastly different things that you might need to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Does everything be encrypted on the client and on the server and in transmission?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And to a certain extent, most of that is...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: is cheaper now than it used to be.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I remember in the days when I first started working at an internet company, we had SSL offloaders because the processing was so great that we had to do all of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now it's just, oops, we used an inch of processing power in the grand scheme of where processing power is today.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it isn't necessarily as difficult.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But you'd want to make sure that you're...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: doing, doing that encryption, but on the other piece, you, you want to make sure when you look at it, it's like, okay, how are we protecting that data?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And what would be the ramifications if this got out?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, if it's traditional note-taking app, the first brush is, I don't know, nobody's really going to care much about that data, but the question is, okay, now who starts using it and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, so now a CEO starts using it because you want CEOs to use it because it might catch on and then the rest of the company might want to use it.

text: And then that's a huge licensing win.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: you know, from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So if that's the case, then you really need to think about it from who's your most secure user that would want to use it and start thinking about it from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Go fix it.

text: So I think there's many ways to deal with encryption.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think there's parts where, for example, making sure that you're over HTTPS and making sure that you only support the most recent versions of TLS, as opposed to just being able to say, okay, we're going to be backwards compatible for everybody.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That may be great for everyone, but it's only going to...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: take off less than 1% of your users, and you're going to have much better protection from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There is encryption that most likely you should be doing on your device, whether that be on the workstation or on a mobile device.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: passwords and GUIDs and all those fun things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You want to make sure that those things are secure as part of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The hacker didn't have to work from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You just gave it to them by doing that.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One question here before we kind of wrap this up is that...
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Um, you mentioned earlier, earlier, something about acquisitions and it's something that a lot of the listeners are going to go through at some point in their career, working in a lot of startups.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: If there's a, if there's a company that's in a startup phase and they know they're going to be going through an acquisition or attempting to do an act, hopefully going to get acquired, whatever they're gonna go down that path.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What would you advise them to take a look at?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Like, hey, make sure you've got these three or four bases covered.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: That's going to kind of put your best foot forward so that they don't run into the situation of like, oh, we're not going to be able to get acquired because of X. Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: I think there's a couple of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: As we talked about earlier, plain text credentials, just get it under control.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's huge, huge, huge in the startup space because I think that the things that you sacrifice from security for speed,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Like in the database?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Like plain text in the database?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: It's usually actually in your code.

text: So it'll be encrypted, and then the person leaves, and there's stuff that's tied to users.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's all sorts of weirdness that tends to happen there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And everybody's different in terms of what it is.

text: But just making sure that even from the database, all credentials should be encrypted, preferably if you're in a cloud service, put it in a vault.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You know, as Azure as Key Vault, it's worth the expense.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's worth the time to do that and do that securely.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The other piece beyond most recent versions of platforms are big.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: um and that and that deficit um when you move miss two three four or five versions becomes so big that it becomes actually almost a monumental effort and then there's all this new stuff that comes out and they're like and it's more secure and they're like oh maybe we should just redevelop it there and and then suddenly redevelopment conversations happen and that's one of the things you want to make sure you avoid is
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: that you want them to really value what you've done, all this work.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and see what it is, because that's the first thing that we have done.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: A third party comes in, who's one of the best in the world that we hired to do it, because, of course, we don't want value of the IP.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But we also expect people to be using that service, to be helping us get to the next generation and help it mature into the next generation of things and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's a huge piece of that puzzle as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's fantastic advice, Glenn.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: I really appreciate that.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: And I know a lot of people listening are definitely taking notes right around here because this is an area which a lot of folks in the Silicon Valley and around the world for that most part face when they're working in startups.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: So great info.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: To tell you the truth, I'm not sure what else I can ask.
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: Is there anything that we're missing that you would like to cover in the secure development lifecycle that I haven't asked?
speaker: SPEAKER_03

text: I think there's a couple of things because I actually taught a four-day class on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So obviously in an hour, we're not going to get too far.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I think that there are many steps to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You can look at Microsoft.com.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It has some more in-depth work on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Recognize that it can be scaled to your size as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because it's about making sure you have secure steps and making sure that you start.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Because the problem is a lot of times people see the whole cycle and they go, I can't do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And they walk away.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And the answer is, okay, well, if you start doing threat modeling, you make better security decisions upfront.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Well, a lot of things will happen down the line and you're better off.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you start ensuring that we've got input validation everywhere, you're just better off for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Even just a few of these are much better than doing nothing from that perspective.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So think of it that way.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think the other piece of it is if you work in an organization that has a security team.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: This is the word of advice I usually have is when you work with a security team as a development organization,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You've got the business going like crazy, and you're trying to move into more of a DevOps slash scrum slash whatever you're going to do next process as it continues to evolve.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it's kind of wavy, and it's kind of equated out to the ocean because you never know what's coming after you.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You've got all of these things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: As security teams, we have very solid requirements.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the easiest thing for us to do is send you the requirements.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we throw you a concrete block.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: In the middle of the ocean, not the most helpful thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But recognize that as a security team, if you can help them understand what your problems are and where you're at, and if you can show interest in ensuring that you want to ensure the steps are in place...
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: then they're more than happy to work with you.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There's some industry issues in terms of, you know, we have that history of being the guy with the sledgehammer, a guy with the stop sign and all of these other things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And that's not really the way it needs to work anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's very much about being engaged together.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The other piece is it used to be the security teams development lifecycle.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you'd go develop, you know, back in the waterfall days, you'd go develop and then you'd send it to the security team and then they'd do a whole bunch of stuff and then they'd send it back and you could go release.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: that doesn't fly anymore because I can't review anything in 30 seconds.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we have to, we have to, we have, the paradigm is really is change where you need to have security, the security scuffs of the SDL, the secure development life cycle need to be in your software development life cycle and really make it one SDL.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Cause the reality is you're doing it for performance.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You're doing it for, for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm working with teams that have made significant progress over the last couple of years.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They were, they were,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: new, we started doing this work with them in a scrum fashion, and suddenly they're doing the threat models themselves.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They understand how to do those things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They're ensuring that they're asking real tough security questions because they start with that thought of process of, oh, I threat modeled in a restaurant and I could get there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's like somebody said, oh, somebody could throw a pipe bomb into the toilet.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Well, most restaurants don't worry about that, but that's out of the box thinking.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: A paratrooper could fall down from the sky and land on the roof.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But those are all things that in theory could happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But the question is, can you put that type of thinking into your own software as you're going through and reviewing your architectural design and say, oh, I don't think anybody could do this, but could someone?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What are the new vectors of attack?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What are you hearing out there?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: One of the things that we flagged teams on recently is the fact that their emails that come from their software come from the external.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It turns out they look a lot like malware.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And look a lot like malicious emails out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So again, you want to make sure that your users are comfortable with everything that you're doing from that perspective because you don't want your emails to be caught in a spam filter and all those other things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You want it to function correctly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And at the same time, you also want to make sure that if somebody takes advantage of your system and pretends to be you, that it's obviously not you.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the more buttoned up you are, the less likely you are to have your users fall for some of the other things because they'd have to go a much further distance to be able to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's great advice.
